coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots derived from platelet graphite nanofibers by liquid-phase exfoliation
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Shih, Yu-Wen
Tseng, Guann-Wei
Hsieh, Cheng-Yu
Li, Yuan-Yao
Sakoda, Akiyoshi
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were formed by liquid-phase exfoliation in an ultrasonic bath using platelet graphite nanofibers (PGNFs) as the raw material and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) as the exfoliation agent. PGNFs, carbon fibers with graphene nanosheets stacked perpendicular along the fiber axis, were sectioned layer by DMSO for 3h with ultrasonic assistance to create GQDs. The diameters of the derived GQDs were in the range of 10–30nm and fewer than five graphene layers were formed. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis revealed that no functional groups were grafted onto the GQDs during the process, indicating that the synthetic route produced high-quality GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Platelet graphite nanofibers
Exfoliation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of PGNFs; (b) HR-TEM image and electron diffraction pattern of PGNFs inset; and (c) graphene layer spacing of the PGNFs.
TEM images of GQDs: (a) a TEM image; (b) a high-resolution TEM image; (c) the edge structures of GQDs; and (d) the CC bond length of GQDs (the inset is a FFT pattern).
(a) AFM image analysis of GQDs and (b) height profile along the black line in (a).
XPS analysis of (a) PGNFs and (b) GQDs.
Proportions of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surfaces of PGNFs and GQDs.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots derived from platelet graphite nanofibers by liquid-phase exfoliation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Chung Cheng University, Chia-Yi 62102, Taiwan, Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate Institute of Opto-Mechatronics, National Chung Cheng University, Chia-Yi 62102, Taiwan, Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Institute of Manufacturing with High-Tech Innovations, National Chung Cheng University, Chia-Yi 62102, Taiwan, Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Industrial Science, University of Tokyo, 4-6-1 Komaba, Meguro-ku, Tokyo 153-8505, Japan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Chung Cheng University, Chia-Yi 62102, Taiwan, Republic of China. Tel.: +886 5 2720411; fax: +886 5 2721206.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were formed by liquid-phase exfoliation in an ultrasonic bath using platelet graphite nanofibers (PGNFs) as the raw material and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) as the exfoliation agent. PGNFs, carbon fibers with graphene nanosheets stacked perpendicular along the fiber axis, were sectioned layer by DMSO for 3h with ultrasonic assistance to create GQDs. The diameters of the derived GQDs were in the range of 10–30nm and fewer than five graphene layers were formed. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis revealed that no functional groups were grafted onto the GQDs during the process, indicating that the synthetic route produced high-quality GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Ever since Geim and Novoselov used scotch tape exfoliation to produce graphene [1], the material has been extensively studied in terms of its formation, characteristics and applications. Graphene has unique characteristics, including electron mobility that exceeds 15,000cm2 V−1 s−1 at room temperature, extremely low electrical resistance (10−6 Ω·cm), excellent thermal conductivity (5300Wm−1 K−1), and high transparency (absorbing only 2.3% of light) [1]. In general, methods for producing graphene can be classified as either physical or chemical. Physical methods include mechanical exfoliation [2] and ultrasonic methods [3,4]. Chemical methods include chemical vapor deposition [5,6] and the redox method [7–9]. Because graphene lacks certain optical properties, such as photoluminescence, its optical and optoelectronic applications are limited.

Ultrasonic methods have been extensively used for the formation of graphene. Hernandez et al. first produced single-layer graphene of various sizes through the ultrasonic liquid-phase exfoliation of graphite [3]. The diameters of the resulting graphenes ranged from a few micrometers to a few tens of micrometers. This result is mainly attributed to the use of n-methyl-2-pyrrolidione (NMP), which has a surface tension of 40.1mJm−2, as a dispersant. The surface tension of NMP is similar to the van der Waals force between graphite layers. Ultrasonic energy effectively drives NMP molecules into graphite layers; consequently, graphene can be derived by exfoliating graphite layers. This method differs from the Hummer method typically employed to produce graphene. The traditional Hummer’s method uses strong acids and strong oxidants to oxidize graphite layer edges and thus increase the spacing of graphite layers, producing graphene oxide after exfoliation. Strong reductants are then used to remove the oxygen-containing functional groups on the graphene surface. This reduction process creates numerous defects on the graphene surface, and these defects influence the electronic properties of the graphene. In contrast, the graphene derived via the ultrasonic liquid-phase exfoliation of graphite does not require a reduction process since there are no oxygen-containing functional groups on its surface, and therefore this form has fewer surface defects and more stable electronic properties.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and graphene nanoribbons have unique optical properties and have thus attracted substantial research attention. Quantum dots are composed of a small number of atoms. When a material is smaller than a certain size in three dimensions, the size of the material limits the movement of electrons, a phenomenon known as the quantum confinement effect (QCE). According to band-gap studies, the QCE becomes more apparent with decreasing graphene size [10–12]. In addition to the QCE, the zigzag and armchair edges of GQDs affect the material properties.

GQDs have high biocompatibility, low toxicity, and photoluminescence, and can be employed in biomedical applications, such as bioimaging [13], disease detection [14], drug delivery [15], photothermal therapy [16] and biosensors [17]. Regarding electronic properties, GQDs have a high electron mobility, similar to that of graphene. They have thus been employed as sensitizers in dye-sensitized solar cells [18]. The composite materials of GQDs and carbon nanotubes, which enhance electronic conversion efficiency, can substitute transition metals in surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [19]. In addition, the optical properties of GQDs can be tuned by changing their functional groups.

Methods for synthesizing GQDs can be classified into two types: top-down and bottom-up. With top-down methods, such as the hydrothermal approach [20] and chemical carbon nanofiber cutting [21], a large graphene sheet is cut into small GQDs using physical or chemical methods. With bottom-up methods, such as solution chemistry [22–24], small molecules are used as precursors to synthesize GQDs through a series of chemical reactions. Other methods, such as electron beam lithography [25] and the ruthenium-catalyzed C60 transformation method [26], require special equipment and complex procedures, and thus have numerous limitations when used to synthesize GQDs.

The present work adopts platelet graphite nanofibers (PGNFs), instead of graphite or graphene oxide, as the starting material to synthesize GQDs. PGNFs are one-dimensional nanostructures formed from parallel stacks of graphene sheets, and have a diameter of 10–40nm. GQDs are obtained by exfoliating graphene layer by layer from PGNFs through liquid-phase exfoliation, which is different from the hydrothermal method commonly used for making GQDs. Unlike the hydrothermal method, which requires a dialysis bag to filter and control the size of GQDs, the size of GQDs can be directly controlled by the size of PGNFs used. Furthermore, PGNF exfoliation does not involve chemical reactions, thereby producing low-defect unfunctionalized GQDs.

PGNFs were obtained by pyrolysis of polymer with the assistance of Ni catalyst [27–29]. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) is used as the dispersant and sectioning agent in the liquid-phase exfoliation. 1mg of PGNFs and 10ml of DMSO solvent were mixed in a glass vial. The sample was then placed in an ultrasonic bath (400W, 40kHz) for 3h to achieve a suspension and cloudy solution. Next, the solution was centrifuged at 6000rpm for 10min to remove larger particles and obtain a clear suspension. Subsequently, a 220nm micropore filtration membrane was employed to remove residual particles. Well-dispersed GQDs were obtained in the clear suspension.

Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM; Hitachi, S-4800) was used to observe the surface morphology of the PGNFs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM; FEI, Tecnai F20 G2F) was employed to observe the internal structure of PGNFs and GQDs, and aberration-corrected TEM (CFEG, JEM-ARM200F) was used to examine the edges of the GQDs and the length of the carbon–carbon bonds in the GQDs. Atomic force microscopy (AFM; Seiko, SPA400) was used to observe the number of GQD layers. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Kratos, Axis Ultra DLD) and Raman spectroscopy (Horiba, XploRA) were employed to analyze the functional group on the GQDs and the characteristics of GQDs, respectively.

The SEM image in Fig. 1 a shows high-purity PGNFs with diameters of ∼10–40nm and zigzag-like surfaces [27]. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image and electron diffraction pattern of the PGNFs in Fig. 1b indicates that the internal structure comprises graphene sheets stacked in parallel, and that the PGNFs grew along the (002) graphite direction. Fig. 1c shows that the d-spacing of the PGNFs is 0.338nm, similar to that of natural graphite layers (0.335nm).

Fig. 2 a shows a TEM image of the GQDs produced using DMSO as a dispersant. As can be seen, DMSO effectively exfoliated the PGNFs to produce GQDs, which were evenly dispersed in DMSO. The diameter of the GQDs ranges from 10 to 30nm, consistent with the diameter of the PGNFs. Fig. 2b shows an HR-TEM image of a GQD. It can be seen that the GQD has an irregular shape and good crystallinity. The lattice constant for the (1120) lattice fringes of the GQDs was determined to be 0.258nm, similar to that for natural graphite (0.246nm). Fig. 2c shows an HR-TEM image of a GQD, indicating that it had two types of edge structure, namely zigzag and armchair. These results are consistent with those reported in previous research [30]. The HR-TEM image of the GQDs in Fig. 2d shows that the CC bond length in the GQDs is ∼0.146nm, close to the ideal CC bond length for graphite (0.142nm). The inset in Fig. 2d shows a fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern of the images. The hexagonal diffraction pattern verifies that the GQD is single-layer graphene [31].

The above results confirm that the combination of DMSO solvent and ultrasonic technology can produce GQDs. The d-spacing of the PGNFs (0.338nm) approximates that of graphite (0.335nm). Therefore, the van der Waals force between the graphene layers of the PGNFs approximates that of graphite. In general, NMP is employed to exfoliate graphite to produce graphene [3]. In this study, many solvents, including NMP, DMSO, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and γ-butyrolactone (GBL) were evaluated. It was found that DMSO is the best solvent for exfoliating PGNFs for the formation of GQDs. This might be because the size and the stacking sequence of graphene layers in the PGNFs are different from that of the graphite.

Fig. 3 a and b show the AFM analysis of the GQDs. It reveals that the heights of the GQDs are in the range of 0.43–1.87nm, and thus there should be fewer than five GQD layers. In the formation of graphene by the liquid-phase exfoliation of graphite using NMP solvent, residual NMP might remain between the graphene layers, so that the spacing of double-layer graphene sheets exceeds the ideal graphite layer spacing (0.34nm) [3]. The size of NMP molecules should thus be taken into account in the estimation of graphene layers using AFM. This study infers that residual DMSO remained within the GQDs, since the boiling point of DMSO (180°C) is similar to that of NMP (202°C).

Fig. 4 shows the Raman spectrum of the GQDs. As can be seen, the intensity of the D-band (ID) exceeds that of the G-band (IG). The ratio of ID to IG is 1.67. The 2D-band is indistinct. The Raman spectrum of the GQDs is different from that of graphene. This is because the size of the GQDs is in the range of a few to a few tens of nanometers, and the edge structure effect becomes more apparent with decreasing graphene size. The vibrational mode of carbon atoms in edge structures of graphene is regarded as a structural defect. Therefore the intensity of the D-band was higher than that of the G-band [31]. In addition, the 2D-band for GQDs differs from that for few-layer graphene. Generally, few-layer graphene has a size of a few tens of micrometers and a structure that comprises continuous sp 2-bonded carbon atoms. Therefore, a high intensity of the 2D-band can be obtained [33]. In contrast, the GQDs have a large number of edge defects, so the intensity of the 2D-band is low and the band is broad. The Raman spectrum of the GQDs agrees with the results reported in other studies [32].

XPS analysis was performed to observe the bonding configuration of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the GQDs. Fig. 5 a and b show the XPS analysis results for the PGNFs and the GQDs, respectively. The bonding energies (eV) in the carbon 1s orbital (representing the type of bond) were as follows: 284.6eV for CC (sp 2), 285.3eV for CC (sp 3), 268.2eV for CO, 287.2eV for CO, 288.9eV for COO, and 291.0eV for the aromatic structure. Table 1 lists the proportions of various bonds in the PGNFs and the GQDs. As can be seen, the proportion of oxygen-containing functional groups is ∼7% for both the PGNFs and the GQDs, and the main type of bond is the COO bond. This study infers that no additional functional groups were grafted onto the GQDs during the exfoliation process of the PGNFs. In contrast, the GQDs obtained from graphite using Hummer’s method or the hydrothermal method require the reduction of graphene oxide, and thus their XPS results show numerous CO bonds in the graphene oxide and many defects in the graphene after the reduction of graphene oxide [34]. According to the XPS results, the surface of GQDs obtained in the present study has few oxygen-containing functional groups, indicating minimal defects.

This study employed liquid-phase exfoliation to produce GQDs using PGNFs as the raw material. DMSO solvent was found to be a suitable exfoliation agent and dispersant for the GQDs. TEM results show that the produced GQDs have good crystallinity and diameters in the range of 10–30nm. AFM results indicate that the number of GQD graphene layers was fewer than five. The Raman spectrum of the as-produced GQDs is in good agreement with those of GQDs reported in other studies. The XPS results show that the proportion of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of GQDs is 7%, equivalent to that on the surface of the PGNFs. This indicates that the process does not involve chemical reactions, producing GQDs the surface of which has minimal defects.
